title: Grissini
subtitle: Thin Italian breadsticks, crisp the whole way through
excerpt: Hand-rolled Italian breadsticks with a fine-grained crumb and a dry, snappable crust. Mixed with olive oil, rolled to pencil-width, and baked until pale gold. Serve with charcuterie, olives, or leaning out of a glass on the table.
sourceNotes: Grissini originated in Turin in the seventeenth century and appear in Italian culinary literature from at least the 1700s. The form documented in Italian regional cooking manuscripts (thin, oil-enriched, long-baked) is what most Italian households produce today. Elizabeth David, Italian Food (1954), notes the Piedmontese breadstick tradition and its association with the Turin bakers; her description of the hand-rolling method (rolling each stick between the palms and stretching it to the full length of the arm) follows the artisan convention. The recipe here synthesises that public-domain tradition with standard Italian baker's ratios.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly on a second tray.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin for up to five days. They dry further and improve in snap.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. Lower heat gives a paler, drier stick; higher causes uneven browning before the centre is cooked through.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dough that has developed enough gluten to stretch into a thin sheet without tearing when held up to light. For grissini, which are rolled to pencil-width, the dough needs enough elasticity to stretch without breaking. A dough that tears immediately is undermixed; one that stretches to near-transparency with no holes is ready.

content.text: Grissini are one of the simplest yeasted bakes: flour, water, olive oil, and time. The dough is low-hydration, which makes it easy to handle and fast to roll. The long bake at a moderate temperature drives out all the moisture, producing a stick that is crisp from end to end rather than crunchy outside and soft within. The
is the one check worth doing before rolling. Under-developed dough tears as you stretch it; a properly mixed dough rolls to pencil-width cleanly.

Combine the flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl, keeping the salt and yeast on opposite sides before mixing. Add the olive oil and most of the water. Mix to a rough dough, then add the remaining water if needed; the dough should be smooth and not sticky. Knead by hand for 8 to 10 minutes, or 5 minutes with a stand mixer on medium, until the dough is elastic and holds its shape. It will pass the stretch test — a small piece pulled between your hands should stretch thin without tearing. Lightly oil the bowl, return the dough, cover with a plate, and leave at room temperature for 1 hour until puffed and relaxed but not doubled.

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Divide the dough into pieces of about 10 g each (a 300 g batch gives 30 to 36). Roll each piece between your palms, then on an unfloured surface, stretching from the centre outward until you have a stick about 25 cm long and as thin as a pencil. Place on the lined trays with a finger's width between them. Scatter sea salt flakes over the tops if using. Bake for 18 to 22 minutes, until pale gold and completely dry to the touch. They firm up further as they cool; transfer to a rack and leave uncovered.
Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more. An oven thermometer takes the guessing out, particularly for grissini, which need a steady heat rather than a blast.
